behaviors.description: Log entries with matching trace IDs are grouped together regardless of the source microservice
behaviors.justification: Grouping is deterministic logic operating on in-memory data structures. A real failure—entries with the same trace ID not being grouped, or groups being malformed—can be detected by batch testing with synthetic data from multiple microservices. No concurrency primitives, I/O, or real network interactions are required to reveal the core failure.
behaviors.test_description: Create synthetic log entries from multiple microservices with a mix of matching and distinct trace IDs. Pass them to the grouping function and verify: (1) all entries with the same trace ID end up in a single group, (2) entries with different trace IDs are in separate groups, (3) grouping is independent of which service emitted the logs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test is a straightforward unit test of grouping logic that would catch if the grouping algorithm is broken.

behaviors.description: Timeline view orders entries by timestamp and correctly handles clock skew up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: Timestamp ordering with skew tolerance is deterministic logic testable with synthetic data. Real failures—incorrect ordering, failure to handle the 500ms tolerance—can be caught without requiring real distributed clocks or concurrent log arrival from multiple sources.
behaviors.test_description: Create synthetic log entries with timestamps covering: (1) normal sequential arrival, (2) clock skew up to ±500ms, (3) identical timestamps, (4) out-of-order arrivals, (5) timestamp differences at the 500ms boundary (±501ms). For each scenario, verify the timeline orders entries correctly and does not misorder due to clock differences within tolerance.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test covers the ordering algorithm's handling of clock skew without requiring real distributed systems or real time passage.

behaviors.description: Detect latency spikes where p99 exceeds 3× baseline and flag in anomaly report
behaviors.justification: Spike detection is deterministic statistical logic: compute p99, compare to baseline threshold. Real failures—incorrect p99 calculation, wrong threshold application, incorrect reporting—can be caught with controlled synthetic latency distributions. No real load, production data, or infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create synthetic latency datasets with known p99 values: (1) p99 < 3× baseline (should not flag), (2) p99 exactly 3× baseline (boundary condition), (3) p99 well above 3× baseline (should flag), (4) varying baseline values to ensure comparison is correct. Verify anomaly report flags or omits spikes according to the threshold rule.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test exercises the detection algorithm directly without needing real production load or data distribution.

behaviors.description: Skip malformed JSON entries without crashing the pipeline; report accurate count of skipped entries
behaviors.justification: Error handling and counting are deterministic. Real failures—crash on malformed JSON, silent drops, incorrect skip count—can be caught by feeding deterministic test cases (various malformed inputs) and verifying behavior. No concurrency, I/O, or infrastructure needed to test the core logic.
behaviors.test_description: Feed the pipeline a mix of valid and malformed JSON entries: (1) missing required fields, (2) invalid JSON syntax, (3) wrong data types, (4) truncated/incomplete entries, (5) null values where forbidden. Verify: (1) pipeline completes without exception, (2) all valid entries are processed correctly, (3) skipped entry count equals the number of malformed entries provided.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test verifies error handling robustness with deterministic cases; no concurrency or volume testing needed at Unit level.

behaviors.description: Handle a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about throughput and performance under load—testing requires volume and timing constraints that cannot be expressed at Unit level. Real failures (processing too slowly, buffer overflow, entries dropped, backlog accumulation) are only revealed when the full pipeline processes high-frequency data. Unit tests cannot measure throughput. Integration-level load testing (wiring data source → processing → output with synthetic high-volume data) can catch whether the system meets the throughput requirement.
behaviors.test_description: Generate 10,000 synthetic log entries and feed them to the ingestion and processing pipeline at a controlled rate of 10,000 per second (or inject them in rapid succession to simulate a burst). Measure: (1) total entries output/processed, (2) processing latency per batch, (3) any gaps or skipped entry numbers. Verify all 10,000 entries are successfully processed, none are dropped, and the system does not fall behind (output rate matches input rate within acceptable latency bounds).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This is a load test of the full pipeline, which is necessary to reveal throughput bottlenecks. A Unit test cannot measure whether 10,000 entries/second is achievable.
